mechanism: Observations are grouped into latent classes based on measured variables (knowledge, attitudes, religiosity) using a probabilistic model that assigns each observation to hidden categories. The model estimates class proportions and class-conditional distributions, typically via maximum likelihood. Regression models then test whether class membership predicts an outcome variable, controlling for covariates.

Observations of individuals' evaluations are modeled as functions of policy positions (left-right coordinates of respondents and parties). Differential item functioning (DIF) correction adjusts for systematic rating differences across groups. Regression or linear models quantify the relationship between policy distance and evaluation scores, with hypothesis tests determining significance.

Voting outcomes are modeled as functions of perceived economic performance, mediated by subnational regime type (competitive vs. dominant-party). A comparison of regression coefficients (or effect sizes) across subnational contexts tests whether performance-based linkages are suppressed in dominant-party environments. Interaction terms capture differential effects by regime type.

Behavioral outcomes (risk-taking choices) are measured in response to manipulated visual stimuli (high vs. low elevation viewpoint). Proposed psychological mediator (illusory sense of control) is measured. Direct effects of elevation and indirect effects through the mediator are estimated using regression, with mediation analysis quantifying the proportion of total effect flowing through the proposed mechanism.

Ad type (progression vs. before/after) is experimentally manipulated as a treatment. Outcome measures (credibility, persuasion) are recorded. Process simulation is hypothesized as a mediator. Moderation effects of skepticism and focus (quick results vs. thorough) are tested via regression with interaction terms. Mediation is tested conditionally on moderators.

Price perception is modeled as a function of stimulus type (side-by-side vs. recalled) and digit position. Comparison magnitude estimates are recorded and analyzed via regression. The mechanism proposed is cognitive representation type (perceptual vs. conceptual); representation is hypothesized to mediate the effect of stimulus type on digit bias. Heterogeneous price data from a scanner panel validate the experimental findings.

Message source (personal vs. impersonal) influences willingness to engage in connective action, mediated by cognitive elaboration. Source credibility moderates the indirect effect. Direct effects on engagement from personal involvement and self-efficacy beliefs are also estimated. All paths are estimated via regression or structural equation modeling.

Survey respondents rate the prevalence of cognitive and motivational biases in themselves versus others. Comparisons are made across self, average person, and reference groups via paired assessments or mean differences. Availability and self-enhancement mechanisms are proposed; one experiment tests whether bias awareness eliminates the effect by providing participants with bias descriptions before reassessment.

Attitudes and emotions toward a group are measured as outcomes. Group stereotypes (positive or negative) and presence/absence of realistic threat context are experimentally varied. Mediation analysis tests whether stereotype endorsement predicts attitudes mediated through threat perception. Moderation is tested, with separate tests in lab and field settings.

Statements are presented in either concrete or abstract language, and participants rate truth likelihood. Mediating variable (vividness perception) and moderating variables (mind-set priming, spatial proximity) are manipulated. Effects are estimated via regression with mediation and moderation analysis.

The affect misattribution procedure (AMP) measures priming effects on judgments. Retrospective reports of intentions and experimental manipulations of intention-to-rate versus intention-to-evaluate are compared. Analysis examines whether effect sizes and reliability differ by intent condition and whether manipulation produces systematic differences, testing the hypothesis that intentionality moderates validity.

Interdependence with mothers and maternal pressure are measured via questionnaire. Motivation after failure and relationship strain are outcome variables. Group comparisons (Asian American vs. European American) and regression analysis test whether pressure effect is moderated by interdependence, with significance tests on difference in coefficients across cultural groups.

Construal level (high vs. low) and perceived changeability of a domain are experimentally manipulated. Negative feedback is presented; acceptance or dismissal is recorded. The hypothesis that high construal and high changeability jointly promote acceptance is tested via factorial design or regression with interaction. Underlying mechanisms (cognitive and motivational) are measured.

Criminal behavior etiology (genetic vs. environmental) is experimentally varied. Defense claims, perpetrator mental state, and causal attributions are measured as outcome variables. Mediation and path analysis quantify indirect effects through perceived control and internal attribution, testing whether genetic attribution has countervailing effects on sentencing.

Participants rate the fit between motivational types (promotion vs. prevention) and task types (eager vs. vigilant) across cultural samples (Western and Eastern). Regression analysis tests whether fit recognition is culture-universal and whether a promotion bias exists. Moderation by independence/interdependence is examined; regression coefficients are compared across cultural groups.

A calibration group estimates quantities, generating a reference distribution. Anchored subjects judge anchor-to-truth comparisons then estimate; estimates are transformed to percentiles in the calibration distribution. Anchoring effect size is quantified as deviation from the neutral (50th) percentile. Relationship between anchoring and subjective confidence is analyzed via correlation or regression.

Native speakers recall exemplars (male and female) in response to generic noun phrases in different grammatical forms (masculine vs. other). Frequency of male vs. female exemplar retrieval is compared across generic types via contingency analysis or logistic regression, testing whether grammatical gender influences cognitive exemplar representation.

Multilingual learners are grouped by source language pairs (L1/L2/L3 combinations). Adjectival interpretation is tested, and the pattern of correct versus incorrect responses is classified as transfer from L1, L2, or neither. Typological proximity is measured between the source and target languages. Regression or classification analysis tests whether typological distance predicts transfer probability.

Blood glucose levels (a biomarker of energy availability) are measured as a continuous variable. Participants make temporal choices between smaller-sooner and larger-later monetary rewards; choice data are converted to discounting rates. Regression analysis tests whether glucose levels predict discounting rates, controlling for covariates. A randomized intervention (sugar vs. artificial sweetener drink) tests causal effects.

Superstition activation (via saying, action, or lucky charm) is experimentally manipulated. Performance outcomes (golf, motor task, memory, anagrams) are measured. Perceived self-efficacy is hypothesized as a mediator; task persistence is measured as an additional pathway. Mediation analysis quantifies the indirect effect through efficacy, and simple mediation tests the effect on performance through persistence.
